trajectory_summary: Rong opened warm and curious — a legitimately engaged first-time solar lead on a large property — and ended the call in a vague 'touch base tomorrow' with no appointment, no bill collected, an unresolved roof anxiety raised twice, a borderline credit concern left dangling, and a request to be referred to a competitor that Randy accepted without a redirect. The call's shape is energy leaked: Randy's verify work is competent but the moments where energy was available — the net-metering aspiration, the ground-panel curiosity, the credit story — were processed as information rather than levers. The outcome is fragile callback at best.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag 'callback' is accurate as a label but understates the fragility. This is not a scheduled callback with a locked time — it's 'I'll send you a phone number, you call them, then let me know or I'll call you.' There is no appointment, no confirmed time, no commitment from Rong to do anything. The closer-applicable handoff is genuinely at risk of ghosting. A more precise tag would be 'fragile_callback_no_anchor.'

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Callback anchor discipline — lock the time or lose the lead
primary_coaching_focus.quote: If you're open to it, we can try to give them a call. I'm not sure if they're still open. Or you can just you can try to give them a call, and then we can kinda touch base tomorrow.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Randy ends a 14-minute call on a warm, large-property, first-time solar lead with no locked callback time, no verbal handshake, and a homework assignment for Rong to complete. On a paid lead, the bill is required before the appointment can be set — that constraint is real — but the absence of a specific 'I'll call you at X tomorrow' anchor means this callback has no structural reason to happen. Note: if this is an organic lead (bill not required for appointment), the bill deferral itself was unnecessary and the appointment could have been set today. The manager should verify lead type before the 1-on-1 and coach accordingly: if paid, the issue is the anchor; if organic, the issue is that Randy deferred a booking he could have made on the spot.

key_moments.quote: This house was built by Toll Brothers in 02/2005. So it's already twenty years. And, I so far, I don't have any problem whatsoever, but but still, you know, it's time getting there.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the largest schema gap in the call. The codex sets a hard DQ threshold at roof age over 15 years with no replacement planned — Rong's roof is 20 years old and he's already articulating that 'time is getting there.' Randy neither flags the potential DQ, explores whether a replacement is on the horizon, nor bridges to ION's in-house roofing as a solution. Instead he moves directly to the warranty deflection. If this is a paid lead, a 20-year asphalt shingle roof is a structural qualification problem the specialist will discover at the proposal stage — a wasted sit. If Rong is open to a re-roof (which his 'time getting there' language hints at), there's an immediate roofing revenue conversation AND the solar install flows cleanly behind it. Randy walked past both the DQ risk and the cross-sell.

key_moments.quote: Since my yard, the backyard, you know, start from, the morning sun all the way to the, you know, the afternoon sun... would you do some extra panel solar panel on the yard, on my you know, the backyard?
key_moments.stewart_read: Ground-mount panels are not an ION offering, so Randy's 'just roof panels' answer is scope-correct. But the enthusiasm signal underneath is significant: Rong is imagining a larger system than a standard roof install, has clearly been researching solar (he knows California companies do ground-mount and net metering), and is mentally ready to go big. Randy processes this as a feature inquiry to deflect rather than as a buying signal to anchor on. The correct move here was to validate the ambition and redirect it: 'The fact that your backyard gets that much sun is exactly why this home is worth designing — the roof panels alone should be able to capture a significant portion of that exposure. The specialist will model it.' That bridges Rong's enthusiasm into the appointment without scope creep.

key_moments.quote: If you have those type of company, forward to me so I can, you know, talk to them, see how many panels can be, set up on the backyard.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a harder shopping signal than it appears. Rong is not asking Randy to help him evaluate ION — he's asking Randy to help him find a competitor. Randy's response ('Okay. Okay. Sounds good. Awesome. I'll put that down in the notes here.') accepts the frame entirely. There is no redirect, no curiosity creation, no acknowledgment that the ground-panel aspiration could be explored further in the specialist call. The codex's comparison framing rebuttal ('it's always best to get a couple looks — we offer different equipment') was available here and unused. Randy logs it as a note and moves on, which tells Rong that ION has nothing to say about this.

key_moments.quote: I think, my credit is somewhere there. I had a seven ten last year, but, because I tried to get a finance from the Bank of America and the PNC Bank... I get rejected because the insufficient revenue. But that hurt my credit score. So my credit score now is 640 to 680.
key_moments.stewart_read: Rong self-reports a range of 640-680, which brackets the 670 threshold on both sides. Randy's response ('I see. I did that to you. You know, if you think that that's the issue, so at least drop the ball') is garbled — it appears to be a transcription artifact of Rong speaking, not Randy's reply, based on the flow. What matters is that Randy neither hard-DQs nor advances. The codex's spencer_operator_note is directly applicable: stated credit is not pulled credit, Vantage scores on consumer apps differ from bureau FICO, and a customer who says '640-680' may be a 690 on the pull. The correct handling depends on ION's unresolved policy question (does the setter DQ on stated, or does the closer's pull decide?), but at minimum Randy should have acknowledged the ambiguity and kept the door open rather than leaving the concern unaddressed.
